This phenomenon aligns with our findings: severe heterogeneity undermines the normality of likelihood-based estimators. 6.4. Horse-racing Data. We apply the proposed likelihood-based estimators to the Hong Kong horse-racing data1. This dataset contains horse-racing competitions from the year 1999 to the year 2005. We cleaned the dataset by removing horses that either participated in too few competitions (fewer than 10) or had won/lost in all the competitions they participated in. After preprocessing, there are 2,814 horses and 6,328 races. We present the information on the top-10 horses based on the estimation from the QMLE, including the number of races, the average place in the race, the estimated utility score, and the estimated 95% confidence interval. As can be seen in Table 4, the identified horses by the QMLE performed consistently well with their historical record. 7. Summary. In this paper, we studied several likelihood-based estimators for utility vector estimation in the PL model. We showed that these estimators can be interpreted from a unified perspective through their estimating equations. Based on this, we established both uniform consistency and asymptotic normality of the estimators under appropriate conditions characterized by the underlying comparison graph sequence; we also discussed the trade-off between statistical efficiency and computational complexity for practical uncertainty quan- tification. For several common random sampling scenarios, such as NURHM and HSBM, the proposed conditions hold optimally in terms of the leading-order graph sparsity. To the best of our knowledge, the asymptotic results in this paper are amongst the first in the PL model that apply to nonuniform edge sizes and heterogeneous comparison probabilities while covering different likelihood-based estimators.ANALYSIS OF LIKELIHOOD-BASED ESTIMATORS IN THE PL MODEL 25 There are several directions worth further study. First, although the proposed conditions are near-optimal in terms of graph sparsity for homogeneous random graphs models, it re- mains unclear whether they are also optimal in terms of graph heterogeneity, which is empir- ically suggested to be appropriately controlled in order for the asymptotic normality result to hold (see Section 6.2). In addition, our uniform consistency results provide a simple condition purely characterized by the underlying comparison graph sequence. It would be interesting to investigate if such conditions hold for other random graph models with prescribed degree structures. Furthermore, since we have provided the asymptotic normality of the likelihood- based estimator of the PL model, a natural step forward is to consider constructing efficient hypothesis testing procedures on the parameter space. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: The paper provides a unified asymptotic theory for likelihood-based estimators (full, marginal, and quasi-likelihood) in the PlackettвҖ“Luce (PL) model, addressing the challenge of heterogeneous comparison graphs and varying comparison sizes. It establishes both uniform consistency and asymptotic normality for these estimators under novel graph topology conditions, including the 'rapid expansion' property and modified Cheeger constant. The analysis highlights a trade-off between statistical efficiency and computational complexity and reveals an 'edge-sharing phenomenon' unique to hypergraphs that affects asymptotic variances. The proposed conditions are shown to be optimal in terms of leading-order model sparsity for common random sampling scenarios like NURHM and HSBM.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: The study employs likelihood-based approaches, including full-likelihood, marginal-likelihood, and quasi-likelihood maximization, for utility vector estimation. Their estimating equations are interpreted through a rank-matching lens. The core theoretical framework involves developing a unified uniform consistency result under graph topology conditions for deterministic designs, extending the chaining technique to hypergraphs. For asymptotic normality, a standard Taylor expansion is utilized, coupled with a truncated error analysis. For the general marginal MLE, this is combined with a leave-one-out perturbation argument to handle the randomness in Hessian matrices. Numerical computations for likelihood-based estimates are performed using the Minorization-Maximization (MM) algorithm.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: Numerical experiments are conducted using synthetic data generated from Nonuniform Random Hypergraph Models (NURHM) and Hypergraph Stochastic Block Models (HSBM) to verify uniform consistency and asymptotic normality. For consistency, n ranges from 200 to 1000, with total edges around 0.1n(log n)^3. For normality, edges are around 10n^1.2. Utility vectors are uniformly sampled in [-0.5, 0.5]. Metrics include average в„“вҲһ error, estimated standard deviation, and coverage probability of 95% confidence intervals. The influence of graph heterogeneity on asymptotic normality is explored in HSBM. The Quasi-Maximum Likelihood Estimator (QMLE) is applied to a real-world Hong Kong horse-racing dataset (1999-2005), comprising 2,814 horses and 6,328 races with non-uniform comparison sizes (4-14). All experiments are run on an Intel i7-4790 CPU.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: A significant limitation is the computational cost of the full Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE), which becomes prohibitive for moderate to large comparison edge sizes (e.g., M=14 in horse-racing data), necessitating a trade-off with statistical efficiency. While the framework accommodates heterogeneous graphs, the optimality of the proposed conditions concerning graph heterogeneity for asymptotic normality remains unclear, with numerical results suggesting that severe heterogeneity can undermine normality. The generalizability of the graph topology conditions (e.g., rapid expansion property) to other random graph models with prescribed degree structures is also an open question. Additionally, the Quasi-MLE, by design, ignores dependence among pairwise comparisons from the same edge, though its impact is argued to be local.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: Future research should focus on investigating the optimality of the proposed conditions in terms of graph heterogeneity for asymptotic normality. Another direction is to explore whether the uniform consistency conditions, particularly the rapid expansion property, hold for other random graph models with diverse degree structures. Furthermore, building upon the established asymptotic normality of the likelihood-based estimators, a natural next step is to construct efficient hypothesis testing procedures on the parameter space of the PL model.

research_study_list.title: Reprompting: Automated Chain-of-Thought Prompt Inference Through Gibbs Sampling
research_study_list.full_text: Open-Vocabulary Calibration for Fine-tuned CLIP Shuoyuan Wang1 2 вҖ  Jindong Wang3 Guoqing Wang4 Bob Zhang 2 Kaiyang Zhou 5 Hongxin Wei1 Abstract Vision-language models (VLMs) have emerged as formidable tools, showing their strong capability in handling various open-vocabulary tasks in im- age recognition, text-driven visual content genera- tion, and visual chatbots, to name a few. In recent years, considerable efforts and resources have been devoted to adaptation methods for improv- ing the downstream performance of VLMs, par- ticularly on parameter-efficient fine-tuning meth- ods like prompt learning. However, a crucial as- pect that has been largely overlooked is the con- fidence calibration problem in fine-tuned VLMs, which could greatly reduce reliability when de- ploying such models in the real world. This paper bridges the gap by systematically investigating the confidence calibration problem in the context of prompt learning and reveals that existing calibra- tion methods are insufficient to address the prob- lem, especially in the open-vocabulary setting. To solve the problem, we present a simple and effec- tive approach called Distance-Aware Calibration (DAC), which is based on scaling the temperature using as guidance the distance between predicted text labels and base classes. The experiments with 7 distinct prompt learning methods applied across 11 diverse downstream datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of DAC, which achieves high ef- ficacy without sacrificing the inference speed. Our code is available at https://github.com/ml- stat-Sustech/CLIP Calibration. вҖ Work done while working at SUSTech as a visiting scholar. 1Department of Statistics and Data Science, Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen, China. 2Department of Computer and Information Science, University of Macau, Taipa, Macau 3William & Mary, Williamsburg, Virginia, USA4School of Computer Science and Engineering, University of Electronic Sci- ence and Technology of China, China. 5Department of Computer Science, Hong Kong Baptist University, Hong Kong SAR, China. Correspondence to: Hongxin Wei <weihx@sustech.edu.cn>. Proceedings of the 41 st International Conference on Machine Learning, Vienna, Austria. PMLR 235, 2024. Copyright 2024 by the author(s). 1. Introduction Vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) have achieved strong performance in open-vocabulary image recognition by leveraging natural language supervi- sion (Radford et al., 2021). To strengthen performance for VLMs in downstream applications, numerous fine-tuning methods have been studied in the literature, among which prompt learning (Zhou et al., 2022b) has gained the most at- tention due to its parameter-efficient design and robustness. Ideally, in an open-vocabulary setting a fine-tuned VLM should make accurate and reliable predictions on both seen (base) and unseen (novel) classes. While prompt learning algorithms can achieve considerable improvement in accu- racy, the reliability issue is still underexplored, preventing the deployment of such models in high-stakes applications like medical diagnosis and autonomous driving. Existing research has shown that the pre-trained CLIP model is well-calibrated in the zero-shot setting (Minderer et al., 2021). However, after fine-tuning for downstream tasks, the model struggles with miscalibration, i.e., the predicted class probabilities do not align with the true correctness likelihood (Oh et al., 2023). Existing research has only studied the calibration of fine-tuned CLIP on base classes. In this work, we present the first study on calibrating fine-tuned VLMs under an open-vocabulary setting. Our experiments reveal that fine-tuned VLMs exhibit overconfidence in novel classes but underconfidence in base classes. Moreover, we find that the current state-of-the-art post-hoc calibration methods can mitigate the miscalibration problem on base classes. However, the calibration result fails to transfer to novel classes, which motivates us to design post-hoc methods for open-vocabulary calibration. In this work, we show that the miscalibration issue on novel classes can be mitigated through a simple fix to temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017)вҖ”the most popular method for post-hoc calibrationвҖ”by scaling the temperature with class information. Our approach, Distance-Aware Calibration (DAC), is motivated by our analysis on the deviation degree of novel classes. Specifically, we show that prompt tuning leads to a large gap between the text features of base classes and those of novel classes. Furthermore, as the deviation degree of a novel class increases, the model becomes more вҖңconfidentвҖқ in its predictions. 1 arXiv:2402.04655v4  [cs.LG]  14 Jun 2024Open-Vocabulary Calibration for Fine-tuned CLIP Concretely, the key idea behind DAC is to scale the tem- perature value based on the distance between the text em- beddings of a novel class and those of base classes. This can be achieved by estimating a textual deviation score for each novel class, which quantifies the deviation degree of the corresponding normalized text features. With the class-dependent scaling factors, our method generates high temperatures for those novel classes far away from the base classes, improving the calibration performance under open- vocabulary settings. We evaluate our approach DAC using 7 distinct prompt learning methods applied across 11 diverse downstream datasets. The results show that DAC consistently improves open-vocabulary calibration on all prompt learning methods. For example, DAC achieves an average of 6.84% reduction in Expected Calibration Error (ECE) for CoOp (Zhou et al., 2022b) over the 11 datasets, with maximum reduction reach- ing 16%. The same improvement can be achieved for other prompt learning methods as well, such as MaPLe (Khat- tak et al., 2023a) and PromptSRC (Khattak et al., 2023b). Beyond prompt tuning methods, we also show that DAC can boost the open-vocabulary calibration performance for existing post-hoc methods such as Density-Ratio Calibra- tion (Xiong et al., 2023). As a post-hoc calibration method, DAC can be easily inte- grated with various prompt tuning methods in a plug-and- play manner. Our method is computationally efficient and does not require hyperparameter tuning (See Figure 5). In short, the main contributions of this paper are summarized as follows: вҖў Problem: We find that fine-tuned VLMs often suffer from miscalibration, and existing post-hoc calibration methods often fail in the open-vocabulary setting. вҖў Analysis: We empirically study the correlation be- tween the calibration and the textual distribution gap. We show that after prompt learning, VLMs tend to be overconfident on classes far away from base classes. вҖў Method: We introduce DAC, a simple and effective post-hoc calibration method that rectifies the predicted confidence while maintaining classification accuracy. вҖў Evaluation: Extensive experiments with 7 tuning VLM tuning methods applied across 11 datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. 2. Background Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP). CLIP is a vision-language model that measures the alignment between image and text, which enables zero-shot inference for open-vocabulary classes (Radford et al., 2021). Let П• and Оё denote CLIPвҖҷs image and text encoders, respectively. Given an image instance x вҲҲ Rd and a text label c, the logit function of CLIP can be formulated as: Lclip c (xi) = П„ В· sim (П•(x), ПҲ(tc)) , (1) where П• and ПҲ denote the image and text encoders in CLIP. tc is derived from a hand-crafted prompt like вҖңa photo of a {class}вҖқ, where the вҖң{class}вҖқ is filled with the text label c. П„ is generally set as a constant of 100. For multi-class classification, we predict by selecting the la- bel with the highest probabilities among the label candidate set C = {ci}K i=1, as shown below: cвҲ— = arg max cвҲҲC p(c|x) = arg max cвҲҲC eLclip c (x) PK i=1 eLclip i (x) (2) where p(c|x) is the predicted probability of class c for the instance x. Prompt tuning. To strengthen the CLIP model in down- stream applications, prompt tuning algorithms are nor- mally designed to optimize the context prompt (Zhou et al., 2022b;a) for high efficiency. In particular, CoOp (Zhou et al., 2022b) replace the hand- crafted textual tokens with a set of learnable textual token T = {v1, v2, . . . ,vM }, where M is the length of tokens. Then, the logit function is: Ltuned c (x) = П„ В· sim (П•(x), ПҲ(tвҖІ c)) , (3) where tвҖІ c = [v1, v2, v3, ...vM , c] and c denotes the textual embedding of class c. During tuning, the learnable tex- tual tokens T are optimized to minimize the cross-entropy loss в„“ce with labeled few-shot samples D = {(xi, ci)}N i=1. Formally, the optimization of CoOp is: T вӢҶ = arg min T 1 N NX i=1 в„“ce(p(ci|xi)) To maintain the generalization on the unseen classes within the same task, i.e., open-vocabulary classes, CoCoOp (Zhou et al., 2022a) employs instance-conditional prompts tm(x) = tm + hОё(x), where hОё(В·) is a neural network pa- rameterized by Оё and m вҲҲ {1, 2, . . . , M}. While the tuning methods achieve promising performance, the reliability of the prediction probability p(c|x) is still a mystery for tuned VLMs. We proceed by introducing the calibration in the prompt tuning setting. Confidence calibration. For multi-class classification tasks, vision-language models are designed to produce the class probabilities p(c|x) as defined in Equation 2. In ad- dition to high accuracy, it is generally expected for vision- language models to be well calibrated, i.e., the predicted 2Open-Vocabulary Calibration for Fine-tuned CLIP Figure 1.Reliability of fine-tuned CLIP (ViT-B/16) on the Flower102 dataset. ECE: Expected Calibration Error (lower is better). Miscalibration is depicted in pink for overconfidence and purple for underconfidence. class probabilities can faithfully estimate the true probabili- ties of correctness. Formally, a perfectly calibrated model satisfies Pr (cвӢҶ = c | p(c|x) = q) = q, вҲҖq вҲҲ [0, 1]. To quantify the degree of miscalibration, the Expected Cal- ibration Error (ECE) is defined as the difference between accuracy and confidence. With N samples grouped into K bins {b1, b2, . . . , bK}, the ECE is calculated as: ECE = KX k=1 |bk| N |acc (bk) вҲ’ conf (bk)|, (4) where acc (В·) and conf (В·) denotes the average accuracy and confidence in bin bk. 3. An Empirical Study In the literature, it has been shown that pre-trained CLIP is well calibrated in zero-shot inference (Minderer et al., 2021). In this section, we empirically study the calibration performance of tuned VLMs. In particular, we compare the zero-shot CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) with a variety of popu- lar prompt tuning algorithms, including: CoOp (Zhou et al., 2022b), CoCoOp (Zhou et al., 2022a), MaPLe (Khattak et al., 2023a), and ProGrad (Zhu et al., 2023). 3.1. Are fine-tuned VLMs well-calibrated? To showcase the miscalibration in tuned VLMs, we fine- tune the pre-trained CLIP with 7 tuning methods on 11 downstream datasets and use ECE as the calibration metric. The descriptions of compared methods and datasets can be found in Subsection 5.1. The miscalibration of tuned CLIP on the Flower102 dataset is visualized in Figure 1 through reliability diagrams. Intuitively, one might expect that deep models will demonstrate increased confidence in the learned base classes after tuning and tend to make more conservative predictions under open-vocabulary conditions since they are not explicitly learned during the tuning process. On the Table 1.ECE (%) of fine-tuned CLIP with different calibration methods. We use ProDA to fine-tune CLIP-ViT-B/16 on ImageNet- 1K. вҖңZSвҖқ means zero-shot CLIP and вҖңConfвҖқ means confidence score without calibration after tuning. вҖңвҖ“вҖқ means the results are not applicable. вҖңConfвҖқ shows underconfidence in base classes. вҖңTSвҖқ and вҖңDENвҖқ show overconfidence in new classes. ZS Conf TS DEN HB IR MIR Base classes 3.58 4.82 1.94 0.73 4.23 2.09 0.82 New classes 2.09 1.59 3.90 3.86 - - - contrary, we make a counter-intuitive observation thattuned VLMs tend to be underconfident in base classes while prone to overconfident in new classes. We present the detailed calibration results in Appendix C.1. 3.2. Can fine-tuned VLMs be calibrated? To address the above miscalibration in tuned CLIP, we uti- lize commonly-used post-hoc confidence calibration for tuned VLMs and expect to calibrate the model well on both base and new classes. Specifically, post-hoc calibration can be split into two categories: scaling-based methods and binning-based methods. Specifically, regarding the former, we use Temperature Scaling (TS) (Guo et al., 2017) and Density-Ratio Calibration (DEN) (Xiong et al., 2023). For the latter, we use Histogram Binning ((Zadrozny & Elkan, 2001)), Isotonic Regression (IR) (Zadrozny & Elkan, 2002) and Multi-Isotonic Regression (MIR) (Zhang et al., 2020). The complete calibration results are available in Appendix C.2. The calibration results on ImageNet-1K are shown in Table 1, and we can make the following findings: (1) Post-hoc calibration can remedy miscalibration in base classes. As a complement of the observation in the recent works (Tu et al., 2023; Oh et al., 2023), we observe that both scaling-based methods ( e.g., DEN) and bin-based approaches ( e.g., MIR) can effectively reduce miscalibra- tion in base classes. (2) Post-hoc calibration on base classes can not transfer to new classes. For instance, the calibration performance of вҖңTSвҖқ and вҖңDENвҖқ is worse than the model without calibration (вҖңConfвҖқ) or tuning (вҖңZSвҖқ). This phenomenon indicates that simply using the calibra- tor learned in base classes is not optimal for wider unseen distributions (Yu et al., 2022). For bin-based methods, they need the probabilities from base classes as the input, which are incompatible with zero-shot prediction. This limitation leads to an open question: How to calibrate tuned VLMs in open-vocabulary classes? 4. Open-Vocabulary Calibration In this section, we first investigate the open-vocabulary cal- ibration with a visualization of the joint vision-language embedding space and show that the textual gap between the 3Open-Vocabulary Calibration for Fine-tuned CLIP base and new classes is crucial for calibration. Next, we quantify this gap by a distance-based metric for the devia- tion degree of a novel class. We then present our method and its advantages for open-vocabulary calibration. 4.1. Feature Space Analysis In the prompt tuning of VLMs, the primary influence of tun- ing is reflected in the textual features. This fact motivates us to explore how these changes in text features correlate to the miscalibration in the new classes. To understand the open- vocabulary miscalibration in VLMs after prompt tuning, we first analyze it from the joint vision-language representation space. Specifically, following modality gap1 (Liang et al., 2022), paired image-text of the downstream datasets are fed into zero-shot / tuned CLIP and their embeddings are visualized in 2D using singular value decomposition (SVD). As mentioned in (Liang et al., 2022), we observe that im- ages and text are embedded at armвҖҷs length in the shared representation space. In zero-shot CLIP, the embeddings of the same modality are relatively concentrated. However, as is shown in Figure 2b, there is a distinct textual distribution gap between the base and new classes after tuning. Further- more, ECE has been significantly increased, indicating that the model had been miscalibrated. Informally, we hypothe- size that the deviation degree in the textual gap is crucial for open-vocabulary calibration. 4.2. Motivation To verify the hypothesis, we first introduce a distance-based metric proximity to quantify the deviation degree of features. Definition 4.1 (Proximity (Xiong et al., 2023)). Consider a feature z вҲҲ Rd as the embedding of a test sample and the held-out feature embeddings E вҲҲRnГ—d, proximity is a function inversely correlates with the mean distance between the test sample and its K nearest neighbors in held-out sets: P(z, E) = Пғ пЈ« пЈӯ 1 K X xiвҲҲNK(z,E) dist(z, xi) пЈ¶ пЈё, (5) where NK(z, E) denotes the set of K nearest neighbors of z and dist (В·, В·) is a predefined metric for distance calcula- tion Пғ is a non-negative, monotonically decreasing function. This definition implies a greater likelihood that the test sam- ple originates from the same distribution as the held-out set as its proximity increases. In VLMs, we have a set of l2-normalized (Radford et al., 2021) textual embeddings of seen class W = {wj}S j=1. Given an arbitrary class ci вҲҲ Uin the test time and its corresponding class token is ci. (6) Here we use eвҲ’x as Пғ (В·) and l2-distance for dist (В·, В·). We present the correlation between the deviation degree of textual feature and calibration in Figure 3. For each textual embedding of new classes, we can calculate its correspond- ing proximity to the hold-out set of base textual features. We can observe that: Lower proximity correlates with higher confidence and ECE. Specifically, if the given text is far from the base textual distribution, tuned VLM tends to present a higher confidence, which results in worse calibration performance. Similar to the pre-training stage (Liang et al., 2022), con- trastive loss in V-L tuning bridges a connection between visual and textual modality. Each modality will be embed- ded into a relatively concentrated distribution. However, in the test time, if the given class is an outlier from the tex- tual distribution learned in tuning, tuned CLIP may not be closely aligned across modalities and fail to give a proper predicted confidence level. Furthermore, as shown in Figure 3b, commonly used calibration techniques such as Tempera- ture Scaling (TS) do not alleviate but deteriorate this issue. We present more details of the experiment are included in Appendix B.1. This motivated us to modify the confidence level when the prediction exhibits lower textual proximity. 4.3. In this work, we propose a post- hoc method to rectify the predicted confidence level. More specifically, we introduce a textual deviation-based score to scale the temperature value, thereby updating the confidence level to align with the true correctness likelihood. Textual deviation estimation. Ideally, the model is ex- pected to give highly uncertain predictions for examples from novel classes, with relatively low accuracy. With this in mind, we define the score by calculating the deviation distances of novel classes to base classes using tuned CLIP models, compared to those of pre-trained CLIP. Let wi and wвҖІ i be the normalized text features of class ci from the pre-trained and tuned VLMs respectively. The Textual Deviation (TD) score for class ci is formulated as: Оі(ci) = P(wвҖІ i, WвҖІ) P(wi, W) , (7) where W and WвҖІ denote the sets of normalized textual fea- tures for base classes. For example, a low TD score indicates that the corresponding class is more likely to be deviant for the textual distribution of base classes. As demonstrated in Subsection 4.2, tuned VLMs tend to be overconfident in examples from such classes. Thus, we propose to update the confidence level according to the TD score for each class. Calibrated inference. Before evaluation, we first col- lect the TD scores О“ = {qi}K i=1 for each class. Given a test instance x, we first calculate its predicted class ЛҶc = arg maxc p(c | x) using the tuned CLIP model. Fi- nally, the rectified logit is calculated as : Ldac c (x) = Оі(ЛҶc) В· П„ В· sim (П•(x), ПҲ(tвҖІ c)) . (8) where tвҖІ c is the textual token of classc, defined in Equation 3. Here, we rectify the confidence level of the prediction by scaling the temperature value П„ with TD score Оі(ЛҶc). For base classes, we set ОіЛҶc = 1, and our DAC is degraded to vanilla temperature scaling. Thus, our method would not af- fect the calibration performance on base classes. Noticeably, DAC offers several compelling advantages: вҖў Realistic: The calculation procedure of DAC only uti- lizes the information of text labels. Thus, our method does not assume access to the visual information from open-vocabulary concepts, which is challenging to ob- tain in real applications. вҖў Algorithm-agnostic: DAC can easily combine with existing prompt tuning algorithms, consistently im- proving the calibration performance in novel classes. вҖў Easy to use: our method does not require hyperparam- eter tuning (see Table 5) and does not introduce extra computational cost (See Table 6). 5. Experiments In this section, we verify the effectiveness of our proposed DAC with 11 downstream classification benchmark datasets. 5.1. Experimental Setup Benchmark setting. We evaluate the confidence calibra- tion of existing popular prompt tuning methods. Following the few-shot evaluation protocol (Zhou et al., 2022a), the datasets are split into base and new classes. The model is trained only on the base classes in a few-shot setting and we generally report the calibration performance on new classes. Datasets. In this work, we follow the standard base-to- new setting (Zhou et al., 2022b;a) and use 11 image recogni- tion datasets in our experiment. The datasets cover diverse classification tasks. It includes general object datasets like ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009) and Caltech101 (Fei-Fei et al., 2004), alongside fine-grained classification datasets such as OxfordPets (Parkhi et al., 2012), StanfordCars (Krause et al., 2013), Flowers102 (Nilsback & Zisserman, 2008), Food101 (Bossard et al., 2014), and FGVCAircraft (Maji et al., 2013) . For specialized categories, it features SUN397 (Xiao et al., 2010) for scene recognition, UCF101 (Soomro et al., 2012) for action recognition, DTD (Cimpoi et al., 2014) for texture classification, and EuroSAT (Helber et al., 2019) with its satellite images. Results DAC improves open-vocabulary calibration in existing prompt tuning. Table 2 shows the average calibration error of all datasets. For the open-vocabulary calibration perfor- mance, our proposed DAC effectively mitigates the miscal- ibration in various tuning methods across all metrics. For instance, DAC significantly reduces the ECE from 13.84% to 7.00%, which makes CoOp more reliable in terms of pre- dicted confidence. Furthermore, note that DAC is also appli- cable to multi-modal tuning (e.g., MaPLe and PromptSRC). This approach adjusts both vision and language branches and DAC consistently improves the open-vocabulary cali- bration performance of these methods. The results indicate that the textual distribution gap widely exists in various tun- ing methods and our proposed DAC can consistently boost open-vocabulary calibration upon existing tuning methods. Specifically, we measure the gap between confidence and accuracy for each bin on the DTD dataset in Table 3. A negative gap indicates underconfident prediction, and vice versa. Discussion How does DAC perform in full fine-tuning? To eval- uate the flexibility of DAC, we provide additional results with a most recent fine-tuning approach named FLYP(Goyal et al., 2023), where both image and text encoders will be up- dated. Following the official implementation, we fine-tune the model for 10 epochs with a learning rate of 1e-5. As is shown in Table 5, DAC successfully reduces the calibration error of models finetuned by FLYP on open- vocabulary classes, which is consistent with the results of prompt-tuning methods. Furthermore, we observe that fine-tuning methods like FLYP also tend to overfit the base classes on several datasets, making it more challenging to calibrate on open-vocabulary classes. Visual proximity vs. Textual proximity.Proximity plays an important role in trustworthy machine learning (Jiang et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2023; Xiong et al., 2023; Yuksekgonul et al., 2023). Recent works mainly focus on the proximity estimation of the model input and largely mitigate the mis- calibration in supervised learning. However, we show that textual proximity is more important than visual proximity in prompt tuning. To this end, we compare the calibration results and inference efficiency of our proposed DAC on ImageNet-1K dataset with Density-Ratio Calibration (Xiong et al., 2023). We present the results in Table 6. For calibration perfor- mance, our proposed DAC outperforms DEN in the calibra- tion of new classes, and we can achieve better calibration results by combining DAC with DEN. Additionally, we as- sess the efficiency of inference. In terms of computational cost, our approach maintains high efficiency with negligible extra computational burden compared to DEN and the base- Table 6.Calibration performance and inference time on ImageNet- 1K. Overall, our proposed DAC shows performance superiority and inference efficiency on calibration in new classes compared with methods based on image proximity. Textual feature normalization is critical. In DAC, we nor- malize the features from the textual encoder for calculating TD scores. However, the significance of such normalization is not immediately evident. In this ablation, we contrast the performance of textual proximity-based calibration with and without feature normalization. As an illustration, we report the calibration results of tuned CLIP with CoOp. Our results in Figure 7 show that employing normalized features can have a better estimation of class-wise proxim- ity, which achieves better calibration performance than raw features. Previous works (Jiang et al., 2024; Sun et al., 2022; Xiong et al., 2023) demonstrated that feature norms may misestimate the K-nearest-neighbor-based score with Eu- clidean distance. Empirically, we verify that normalization plays a key role in our distance-based calibration. Calibration with data from novel classes. Generally, it is impractical for a user to access the image-text pairs from the novel classes during tuning. As an analysis, we verify the feasibility of open-vocabulary calibration utilizing the knowledge from new classes for post-hoc calibration. To this end, We present the calibration results that the model will be tuned in base classes and calibrated using open- vocabulary image-text pairs, which utilize Temperature Scal- ing (TS) as the post-hoc calibration method. As illustrated in Table 7, TS effectively calibrates the model using image-text pairs of the new classes. However, such calibration compromises the modelвҖҷs ability to deliver a reliable confidence level for the base classes. Given that TS modifies the logits scale with a single scalar, it is infeasible to calibrate the model on both base and new class. Overall, as a training-free calibration, our proposed DAC boosts the calibration performance according to textual proximity. 7. Related work Prompt tuning. Thanks to the rich natural language super- vision, pre-trained large vision-language models (Jia et al., 2021; Radford et al., 2021) can effectively comprehend vi- 8Open-Vocabulary Calibration for Fine-tuned CLIP Caltech101StanfordCarsFlowers102 Food101 SUN397 UCF101 ImageNet 0 5 10 15 20ECE (%) Conf w/ Norm. w/o Norm. Figure 7.Ablation results of textual feature normalization with CoOp. We compare the effect of using normalization in the textual feature vs. without normalization. sual concepts and apply them in downstream tasks (e.g. im- age classification (Radford et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2022b; Lu et al., 2022; Naeem et al., 2023), image captioning (Hu et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023b;a), visual question answer- ing (Parelli et al., 2023)) in a zero-shot manner. Despite VLMвҖҷs effectiveness in generalizing new visual concepts, the performance of zero-shot VLM still lags behind the fine- tuned performance on specific downstream tasks (Zhang et al., 2023). To further boost the downstream adaptation of pre-trained VLMs, many parameter-efficient tuning methods like prompt tuning (Zhou et al., 2022b;a; Yao et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2023; Zang et al., 2022) have been proposed for high efficiency. Recently, several works have explored mul- timodel tuning (Khattak et al., 2023a;b; Shen et al., 2024), which adapts both vision and language branches of CLIP for better modality alignment. Despite the great success of V-L tuning, their effectiveness on safety-related evalua- tion is less explored. In this work, we first investigate the calibration performance for fine-tuned VLMs. We present that fine-tuned CLIP generally suffers from miscalibration, and our proposed DAC can effectively mitigate the issue by rectifying the logit scale based on textual information. Confidence calibration. Confidence calibration has been widely studied to ensure that the confidence levels output by models accurately reflect their true performance. To achieve this, A common practice is to employ post-hoc calibration techniques after model training. Generally, post-hoc calibra- tion can be split into two paradigms: scaling-based methods (Guo et al., 2017; Tomani et al., 2022; Yu et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2020; Joy et al., 2023; Xiong et al., 2023) and bin- based methods (Zadrozny & Elkan, 2001; 2002; Zhang et al., 2020; Roelofs et al., 2022). For scaling-based calibration, a representative method is temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017), which learns a single scalar for rescaling the logit. Mix-n-Match (Zhang et al., 2020) leverages ensemble and composition techniques to achieve data efficiency and main- tain accuracy in confidence estimates. ATS (Joy et al., 2023) modifies the predicted confidence by per-data-point adaptive temperature. For binning-based calibration, the samples are segmented into various bins according to confidence levels, Table 7.Average performance of ECE (%) on 11 datasets. CoOp is used to tune the pre-trained CLIP. TS new / TSbase denotes the calibration on data of base/new classes. Class Conf TS base TSnew DAC Base 2.95 2.57 13.05 2.95 New 13.84 12.95 7.56 7.00 with each bin undergoing calibration individually. Recently, several works have explored calibration in VLMs (Oh et al., 2023; LeVine et al., 2023). In this work, we focus on the open-vocabulary calibration of fine-tuned VLMs. We first show that established post-hoc calibration on base classes can not transfer to new classes. To this end, our DAC fixes the logit scale of prediction via textual deviation-informed score in new classes. 8. Conclusion In this paper, we introduce Distance-Aware Calibration (DAC), a simple fix to temperature scaling in fine-tuned VLMs, that enhances existing prompt tuning methods in open-vocabulary calibration. By scaling the temperature value according to the distance between the predicted text label and base classes, fine-tuned CLIP tends to give a more reliable confidence level for new classes. Extensive exper- iments show that DAC can improve the calibration in new classes after prompt tuning while maintaining the perfor- mance in base classes. Our proposed DAC can be easily incorporated with existing scaling-based calibration without compromising the inference speed. We hope our findings inspire further research on the calibration of VLMs. Acknowledgements This research is supported by the Shenzhen Fundamental Research Program (Grant No. JCYJ20230807091809020). Experimental Setting A.1. Dataset Following the base-to-new generalization setting in CoCoOp (Zhou et al., 2022a), we split the dataset into two subsets base and new. The labels for these subsets do not overlap. As is shown in Table 8, we split the dataset into three folds through our experiments. The pre-trained CLIP will be first tuned on base classes. For former calibration methods like (Guo et al., 2017), they need a calibration set to align the predicted probabilities more closely with the actual likelihoods of the outcomes. Since we can not access new (open-vocabulary) classes, we sample a calibration set from the datasets of base classes which does not overlap with the training set. Finally, we evaluate the model on both base classes and new classes. In our experiments, we mainly focus on the calibration in new classes. Table 8.The split strategy of downstream datasets. Detailed Experimental Results In this section, we illustrate the experimental results in detail. C.1. The calibration performance of tuned VLMs To showcase the miscalibration in tuned VLM, we fine-tune the pre-trained CLIP with 7 tuning methods on 11 downstream datasets and use ECE as the calibration metric. We report the detailed calibration results in this section. The datasets and compared methods are listed in Section 5.1. As is shown in Table C.1, for the calibration of the base class, CoOp shows the best calibration results in base classes. We can also observe that distillation-based tuning (KgCoOp, ProGrad and PromptSRC) restrains the modelвҖҷs confidence in base classes. For the calibration of the new class, distillation-based tuning utilizes zero-shot CLIP as the teacher model to improve the generalization ability, which generates relatively reliable predictions in unseen classes as expected. More importantly, we observe that tuned VLMs tend to be underconfident in base classes while prone to overconfident in new classes. This phenomenon motivates us to further investigate the calibration of fine-tuned VLM. Calibrated performance for tuned VLM We utilize commonly used post-hoc confidence calibration for tuned VLMs and expect to calibrate them well on both base and new classes. We can observe that established calibration can remedy miscalibration in base classes. However, such efficacy can not transfer to new classes. C.2.1. BASE CLASS Post-hoc calibration can remedy miscalibration in base classes. In the latest works (Tu et al., 2023; Oh et al., 2023), scaling-based methods like TS have been successfully adopted in VLM calibration, and their findings reveal that this calibration can handle the miscalibration well in the close-world setting. As a complement, we observe that both scaling- based (e.g., DEN) and bin-based (e.g., MIR) calibration can notably reduce miscalibration in base classes. Post-hoc calibration on base classes can not transfer to new classes. For instance, scaling-based methods like TS scale the logits by a single scalar temperature value. If tuned VLMs show underconfidence in base classes, TS sharpens the logit scale before the softmax function and makes the prediction more confident. As a cost, the confidence distribution of tuned VLM becomes sharper and causes the VLMs to display exaggerated overconfidence in new classes. for bin-based methods, they need the probabilities from base classes as the input, which are incompatible with zero-shot prediction. Accuracy & Confidence of tuned VLMs In this section, we analyze the miscalibration from the classification performance. Since open-vocabulary classes are not explicitly seen during the tuning, some might believe that this miscalibration arises from rapid accuracy decreasing and leads to a larger Expected Calibration Error (ECE). This hypothesis leads to an open question: вҖқShould we blame accuracy degeneration for miscalibration?вҖқ We report the average performance on 11 datasets in Table 13 and 14. Although the classification performance on new classes may be comparable or lower, the average predictive confidence of tuned CLIP is still relatively higher than zero-shot CLIP, which results in a larger ECE. Moreover, we take a close look at the confidence level of MaPLe in new classes. Although the accuracy is comparable with zero-shot CLIP, we can find that the confidence level is greatly improved, which indicates the overconfidence issue. C.3.1. Detailed results of main experiment In this section, We present the detailed results on the calibration of new classes to verify that DAC can open-vocabulary calibration in existing prompt tuning. For comprehensive evaluation, 4 standard metrics are used in our evaluation of open-vocabulary confidence calibration: Expected Calibration Error (ECE) (Guo et al., 2017), Maximum Calibration Error (MCE) (Guo et al., 2017), Adaptive Calibration Error (ACE) (Nixon et al., 2019) and Proximity-Informed Expected Calibration Error (Xiong et al., 2023). Open-Vocabulary Calibration for Fine-tuned CLIP

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: The paper addresses the significant problem of miscalibration in fine-tuned Vision-Language Models (VLMs), particularly in open-vocabulary settings where existing post-hoc calibration methods are insufficient. It empirically demonstrates that fine-tuned VLMs exhibit overconfidence in novel classes and underconfidence in base classes, correlating this miscalibration with a textual distribution gap. The main contribution is the introduction of Distance-Aware Calibration (DAC), a simple, effective, and computationally efficient post-hoc method that rectifies predicted confidence by scaling the temperature based on the textual distance between predicted labels and base classes, thereby improving open-vocabulary calibration without sacrificing inference speed or requiring hyperparameter tuning. Extensive experiments across 7 VLM tuning methods and 11 datasets validate DAC's effectiveness.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: The Distance-Aware Calibration (DAC) method is a post-hoc calibration technique that modifies the temperature scaling approach. It is motivated by the observation that miscalibration in fine-tuned VLMs, especially overconfidence in novel classes, correlates with the textual distribution gap between novel and base classes. DAC quantifies this gap using a Textual Deviation (TD) score, calculated as the ratio of proximity scores for a class's textual embedding from a tuned VLM to its embedding from a pre-trained VLM, relative to the textual features of base classes. Proximity is defined as being inversely correlated with the mean distance to K nearest neighbors in a held-out set of textual features. For inference, the predicted logit for a test instance is rectified by scaling the temperature value with the TD score of the predicted class. For base classes, the TD score is set to 1, making DAC degrade to vanilla temperature scaling. DAC is designed to be algorithm-agnostic, easily integrable with various prompt tuning methods, and computationally efficient.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: The research evaluated DAC using CLIP (ViT-B/16) as the pre-trained VLM, averaged results over 3 runs, and fine-tuned models with 16 samples per class in a few-shot setting. Experiments were conducted on 11 diverse image recognition datasets, split into base and new classes following the standard base-to-new generalization setting: Caltech101, OxfordPets, StanfordCars, Flowers102, Food101, FGVCAircraft, SUN397, DTD, EuroSAT, UCF101, and ImageNet. The study compared DAC's performance with 7 existing VLM tuning algorithms, including prompt tuning methods (CoOp, CoCoOp, ProDA, KgCoOp, ProGrad) and multi-modal tuning methods (MaPLe, PromptSRC). Calibration performance was measured using four standard metrics: Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Maximum Calibration Error (MCE), Adaptive Calibration Error (ACE), and Proximity-Informed Expected Calibration Error (PIECE). The number of nearest textual features (K) for TD score calculation was set to 5, with robustness analysis showing effectiveness for K>1. Additional evaluations included full fine-tuning with FLYP and comparison with Density-Ratio Calibration (DEN).
llm_extracted_info.limitations: The paper identifies that existing post-hoc calibration methods are inadequate for addressing miscalibration in fine-tuned VLMs within open-vocabulary settings, particularly for novel classes. It also notes that calibration performed on base classes often fails to transfer effectively to new classes, and bin-based calibration methods are incompatible with zero-shot predictions due to their reliance on base class probabilities. While DAC addresses these issues for novel classes, it is a post-hoc method, meaning it corrects confidence after prediction rather than influencing the model's learning process directly. Its effectiveness relies on the quality of textual embeddings and the assumption that the textual deviation gap is the primary cause of open-vocabulary miscalibration. The paper also mentions that some full fine-tuning methods, like FLYP, can overfit base classes, making open-vocabulary calibration more challenging.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: The findings encourage further research into the calibration of Vision-Language Models. Potential future directions include developing fine-tuning methods that are inherently better calibrated and less prone to overfitting base classes in open-vocabulary settings. Further exploration could involve investigating the optimal integration of visual proximity information with textual proximity for calibration, especially in different VLM architectures or tasks beyond prompt tuning. Additionally, research could focus on creating more robust and unified calibration solutions that effectively address miscalibration across both base and novel classes simultaneously, without compromising performance on either, building upon DAC's success in open-vocabulary settings.

research_study_list.title: DialCoT Meets PPO: Decomposing and Exploring Reasoning Paths in Smaller Language Models
research_study_list.full_text: Fast modeling of the shear three-point correlation function Sunao Sugiyama ,вҲ— Rafael C. H. Gomes , and Mike Jarvis Department of Physics and Astronomy, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, PA 19104, USA (Dated: July 3, 2024) The three-point correlation function (3PCF) of a weak lensing shear field contains information that is complementary to that in the two-point correlation function (2PCF), which can help improve the cosmological parameters and calibrate astrophysical and observational systematics parameters. However, the application of the 3PCF to observed data has been limited due to the computational challenges of calculating theoretical predictions of the 3PCF from a bispectrum model. In this paper, we present a new method to compute the shear 3PCF efficiently and accurately. We employ the multipole expansion of the bispectrum to compute the shear 3PCF, and show that the method is substantially more efficient than direct numerical integration. We found that the multipole-based method can compute the shear 3PCF with 5% accuracy in 10 (40) seconds for the single (four) source redshift bin setup. The multipole-based method can be also used to compute the third-order aperture mass statistics quickly and accurately, accounting for the bin-averaging effect on the shear 3PCF. Our method provides a fast and robust tool for probing the underlying cosmological model with third-order statistics of weak lensing shear. I. INTRODUCTION In the analysis of wide field galaxy survey data, most of the works have relied on the two-point correlation func- tion (2PCF) to extract cosmological information. The 2PCF is a powerful statistic that characterizes the clus- tering of galaxies or matter through weak lensing, and provides a wealth of information about the underlying cosmological model. In particular, the recent 2PCF anal- ysis of weak lensing survey data put a tight constraint on the matter clustering parameter S8 вүЎ Пғ8 p в„Ұm/0.3 at the level of several percent precision [1вҖ“10]. This tight constraint is now getting comparable to the cosmological constraint from CMB data [11], and starts to indicate the so called S8 tension, referring to the discrepancy between the late-time universe probed by galaxy surveys and the early universe probed by CMB data. However, the 2PCF is limited in its ability to capture the full information content of the weak-lens shear field. If the shear field is Gaussian distributed, the 2PCF con- tains the full information of the field, while in reality the field is highly non-Gaussian due to non-linear struc- ture formation in the late-time Universe. Because of this non-Gaussianity of the weak lensing field, the 2PCF is not enough and one needs to use higher-order statis- tics in addition to the 2PCF to capture the full infor- mation of the field, e.g. higher-order moments [12вҖ“20], peak counts [15, 21вҖ“30], one-point probability distribu- tions [31вҖ“33], Minkowski functionals [16, 34вҖ“37], Betti numbers [38, 39], persistent homology [40, 41], scatter- ing transform coefficients [42вҖ“44], wavelet phase harmon- ics [45], and kNN and CDF [46, 47], map-level inference [48, 49], and machine-learning methods [50вҖ“54]. These higher-order statistics offers additional statistical power on the cosmological parameters that the 2PCF constrains вҲ— ssunao@sas.upenn.edu and also offers different sensitivities to cosmological pa- rameters compared to the 2PCF and can be used to test aspects of cosmological models that the 2PCF cannot adequately probe. One of the most natural statistics beyond the 2PCF is the three-point correlation function (3PCF). The 3PCF has been extensively studied in the context of galaxy surveys[55вҖ“57] and it has been shown that the infor- mation content of the 3PCF is complementary to the 2PCF to give a tighter constraint on the cosmologi- cal parameters[41, 58]. With stage-III weak lensing survey data, the shear 3PCF and third-order aperture mass statistics are measured [59, 60] and combined with the 2PCF to more tightly constrain the cosmological parameters[61]. However, the application of the shear 3PCF analysis for cosmology inference has been limited until recently due to the computational challenges as- sociated with measuring the 3PCF from galaxy shape catalogs and calculating its theoretical predictions. The naive computational cost of measuring the 3PCF from the shapes of N galaxies scales as O(N3), making it impractical for large datasets. Porth et al. [60] devel- oped a new algorithm to compute the 3PCF based on the multipole expansion, reducing the computational cost to O(N log N)[62]. This method builds on previous work by [63вҖ“65] on the 3PCF of galaxy clustering, and extends their method to the spin-2 shear 3PCF. The theoretical prediction of the shear 3PCF faces similar computational challenges. The 3PCF is the Fourier transform of the bispectrum, involving a three- dimensional integral. Additionally, the shear field is a spin-2 field, adding complexity due to oscillating phase factors in the integrand, which complicates numerical in- tegration. When we estimate the cosmological param- eters by fitting the theoretical model to the measured 3PCF, we evaluate the 3PCF at вҲј 103-4 Monte-Carlo sampling points in the parameter space, so it is crucial to have a fast and accurate method to compute the the- oretical prediction of the 3PCF. arXiv:2407.01798v1  [astro-ph.CO]  1 Jul 20242 в„“1 в„“2 в„“3 ОІ1 ОІ2 ОІ3 Оұ вҲҶ ОІ FIG. 1. The parametrization of triangle in Fourier space. Because of the statistical isotropy, three vectors satisfyP3 i=1 в„“i = 0, forming a triangle. The polar angle of each Fourier vector, в„“i, measured from the x-axis is denoted by ОІi. The multipole expansion is applied to the inner angle Оұ, measured from в„“1 side to в„“2 side. The outer angle is defined by вҲҶОІ вүЎ ОІ1 вҲ’ ОІ2 = ПҖ вҲ’ Оұ. In this paper, we employ the same idea of a multipole expansion as Porth et al. [60] used for the efficient mea- surement algorithm. This approach has been previously developed and investigated in the context of the galaxy clustering 3PCF [66вҖ“68]. We extend the multipole-based method for theoretical predictions to the 3PCF of spin- 2 shear fields. This multipole-based approach allows for the separation of the opening angle dependence of the triangle in both real and Fourier space, enabling analyt- ical integration over the angle. This method is substan- tially more efficient than direct numerical integration, with evaluations taking only approximately 10 seconds. This paper is organized as follows. In section II, we re- view the basic properties of weak lensing shear statistics. In section III, we present the formalism of the multipole- based method to compute the shear 3PCF efficiently. We also discuss the binning effect of the 3PCF signal. In section IV, we present the results of the multipole-based method: we discuss the stability of the computation, the accuracy of the method, and the computational efficiency compared to existing methods. Finally we conclude the paper in section V. II. THIRD-ORDER STATISTICS OF COSMIC SHEAR A. Connection of shear and matter fields Weak lensing is the effect of the shape distortion of the background image due to the intervening matter density field of large-scale structure. The two most fundamen- tal quantities of weak lensing effect is the convergence Оә and the shear Оі, which quantify the isotropic stretch and shape distortion of the background image respectively. The convergence field at a position X on the sky plane is given by the line-of-sight projection of the matter density contrast Оҙm weighted by the lensing efficiency function, Оә(X) = 3в„ҰmH2 0 2c2 Z вҲһ 0 dПҮ q(ПҮ)Оҙm (ПҮX; z(ПҮ)) a(ПҮ) (1) q(ПҮ) = Z вҲһ ПҮ dПҮвҖІp(ПҮвҖІ)ПҮвҖІ вҲ’ ПҮ ПҮвҖІ , (2) where the lensing efficiency q(ПҮ) is averaged with the source probability distribution p(ПҮ), satisfying the nor- malization RвҲһ 0 dПҮ p(ПҮ) = 1. Weak lensing shear is a spin-2 quantity, which in a Cartesian frame can be de- scribed by the complex form: Оіc = Оі1 + iОі2, with Оі1 and Оі2 denoting the shear components along the x-axis and an axis rotated 90 degrees from the x-axis, respectively. When the shear is projected onto another reference frame which is rotated by О¶ from the Cartesian frame, the shear field follows the spin-2 transformation, Оі(X; О¶) вүЎ Оіt(X; О¶) + iОіГ—(X; О¶) = вҲ’Оіc(X)eвҲ’2iО¶. The convergence and shear fields are related to each other in Fourier space: ЛҶОіc(в„“) = ЛҶОә(в„“)e2iОІ, (5) where ОІ is the polar angle of Fourier mode в„“. The power spectrum and bispectrum of the conver- gence field are defined by вҹЁЛҶОә(в„“1)ЛҶОә(в„“2)вҹ© =(2ПҖ)2ОҙD(в„“1 + в„“2)PОә(в„“1) (6) вҹЁЛҶОә(в„“1)ЛҶОә(в„“2)ЛҶОә(в„“3)вҹ© =(2ПҖ)2ОҙD X3 i=1 в„“i  Г— BОә(в„“1, в„“2) (7) Here the Dirac delta arises because of the statistical uni- formity of the convergence field. Because of this Dirac delta function, the three Fourier vectors forms a trian- gle for the bispectrum, which is depicted in Fig. 1. The dependence of the bispectrum on its Fourier modes can be reduced further by using statistical isotropy and by noting that the triangle can be uniquely identified by the two side lengths and the inner angle between them; to clearly express this dependency, we introduce side-angle- side (SAS) notation as follows. BОә(в„“1, в„“2) = bОә(в„“1, в„“2, Оұ). (8) Note that the inner angle Оұ is related to the polar angles of Fourier vectors в„“1,2 as Оұ = ОІ2 вҲ’ (ОІ1 вҲ’ ПҖ) вүЎ ПҖ вҲ’ вҲҶОІ, (9)3 where вҲҶОІ is the outer angle. Weak lensing produces only E-mode and consequently, the bispectrum due to weak lensing is mirror symmetric, bОә(в„“1, в„“2, Оұ) = bОә(в„“1, в„“2, вҲ’Оұ), (10) meaning that the bispectrum depends on its inner angle Оұ only through its cosine. The convergence power spectrum and bispectrum are related to those of the matter density field under the flat sky and Limber approximations as PОә(в„“) = 3в„ҰmH2 0 2c2 2 Z вҲһ 0 dПҮq2(ПҮ) a2(ПҮ) Г— Pm  в„“ ПҮ; z(ПҮ)  (11) BОә(в„“1, в„“2, в„“3) = 3в„ҰmH2 0 2c2 3 Z вҲһ 0 dПҮ q3(ПҮ) a3(ПҮ)ПҮ Г— Bm в„“1 ПҮ , в„“2 ПҮ , в„“3 ПҮ ; z(ПҮ)  , (12) where Pm and Bm are the matter power- and bispectrum in three dimensional space defined as вҹЁЛҶОҙm(k1; z)ЛҶОҙm(k2; z)вҹ© =(2ПҖ)3ОҙD(k1 + k2) Г— Pm(k1; z) (13) вҹЁЛҶОҙm(k1; z)ЛҶОҙm(k2; z)ЛҶОҙm(k3; z)вҹ© =(2ПҖ)3ОҙD X3 i=1 ki  Г— Bm(k1, k2; z). (14) In this paper, we consider Bihalofit for the model of the matter bispectrum [69]. Bihalofit predicts the matter bispectrum at a given redshift and a set of three co- moving Fourier modes in a given cosmology based on a fitting formula calibrated with N-body simulations. We perform the line-of-sight integration in Eq. (12) to obtain the convergence bispectrum. Throughout this paper, we use the cosmology from Planck 2018, where Пғ8 = 0.8102, в„Ұm = 0.3111, в„ҰОӣ = 0.6889, h= 0.6766. B. Cosmic shear 3PCF Because the shear has two components, there is some freedom in choice of shear components with which we construct the three-point correlation function. Schneider et al.[56] developed the natural componentsof the shear 3PCF. There are four natural components of the shear 3PCF, which do not mix together under any rotations of the triangle. For a fixed shape of triangle, we still have a degree of freedom for the choice of reference point on which we define the shear components, called shear projection. They showed that each of the natural com- ponents is invariant under a change of shear projections up to a phase factor. With arbitrary choice of shear pro- jection P, the natural components of the shear 3PCF are Оё1 Оё2 X1 X2 X3 О¶Г— 2 = П•1 О¶Г— 3 = П•2 О¶Г— 1 = 1 2(П•1 + П•2) ПҶ FIG. 2. Derivation of the formalism In this work we develop the multipole expansion of third-order cosmic-shear statistics. We first focus on the О“Г— 0 , and show the results for other projections later. We start with the bispectrum multipole expansion. The multipoles of the zero-th and first com- ponents have the following symmetry in itself О“Г—,(M) Вө (Оё1, Оё2) = О“Г—,(вҲ’M) Вө (Оё2, Оё1), (44) for Вө = 0 and 1. The above properties of the component multipoles stem from the properties in Eqs. (19) - (22). These are es- pecially useful because we can reduce the computational time by a factor of two just by exchanging the arguments Оё1 and Оё2 in one multipole to get the corresponding mul- tipole for a different component. It is interesting to compare the formalism obtained in this section for weak lensing to the similar formal- ism developed in the context of galaxy clustering [66вҖ“ 68]. In these papers, authors introduced multipole expan- sion using Legendre polynomials both in real and Fourier space, with a focus on the 3PCF of galaxy number den- sity contrast, i.e. the 3PCF of spin-0 scalar fields. The 3PCF multipoles defined in these papers for the galaxy- clustering 3PCF has one-to-one correspondence to the bispectrum multipoles and there is no coupling between the 3PCF and bispectrum multipoles. In contrast, the multipole formalism developed in this paper for the 3PCF of spin-2 shear fields has coupling between the 3PCF and bispectrum multipoles, which essentially originates from the presence of spin-2 phase factors, the second-to-last factor in Eq. (30). The formalism developed here has several advantages for the efficiency of numerical evaluation from a given model of the bispectrum. First, the bispectrum in the context of standard cosmology is usually a smooth func- tion of the inner angle Оұ, and thus the multipole expan- sion is efficient even after truncating the summation at a reasonable choice of maximum multipole Lmax. As we will see later in section IV, we can safely set Lmax вҲј 30 to get a robust estimate of the 3PCF, which enable us quick resummation of bispectrum multipoles. Second, because the function GLM (ПҲ) is independent of any cos- mological/astrophysical model, we can evaluate the func- tional form for all desired L and M as a function of ПҲ prior to the actual time-consuming parameter infer- ence. Third, the integral over two Fourier-mode ampli- tudes в„“1,2 including two oscillating Bessel functions, i.e. double Hankel transformation, can be robustly and ef- ficiently performed using a two-dimensional version of Fast Fourier Transformation on logarithmic scale (2D- FFTLog), which was originally developed for the efficient estimate of covariances of two-point correlation functions [73]. It is noteworthy that the multipole expansion method developed in this paper has synergy with the recent pa- per by Porth et al.[60]. In that paper, they invented an efficient algorithm to estimate the 3PCF based on a mul- tipole expansion with Г—-projection, where the estimator 0 25 (0,0) (0,1) (0,2) (0,3) (0,4) 0 25 (1,0) (1,1) (1,2) (1,3) (1,4) 0 25GLM(ПҲ) (2,0) (2,1) (2,2) (2,3) (2,4) 0 25 (3,0) (3,1) (3,2) (3,3) (3,4) 0 1 4 1 2 0 25 (4,0) 0 1 4 1 2 (4,1) 0 1 4 1 2 ПҲ/ПҖ (4,2) 0 1 4 1 2 (4,3) 0 1 4 1 2 (4,4) FIG. 3. Because of this agreement in the quantities we measure/predict from data/theory, the effect of the fi- nite truncation of multipoles in the multipole expansion can be alleviated by using the same maximum multipoles in the measurement and theory. B. Multipole coupling function In this section, we present the features of the multipole- coupling function GLM (ПҲ) defined in Eq. (35). Fig. 3 shows the behaviour of the function for L = 0, . . . ,4 and M = 0, . . . ,4. The functions with the same L вҲ’ M value have a similar shape for their ПҲ dependence. The overall amplitude of the function decreases asL or M gets larger. Especially by comparing the functions in a column (or row), we can see that the amplitude of the function de- creases as the difference between L and M increases, and the amplitude is largest when L = M. This means that the bispectrum and 3PCF multipoles couple most tightly between similar multipole indices ( L вүҲ M). Lastly, we list other minor but nontrivial features of the multipole-7 coupling function: GLM (ПҲ вҲҲ [0, ПҖ/4]) = 0 for L < M (46) GLM (0) = 0 for L вҲ’ M = 2n (47) GLM (ПҖ/4) = 0 for L вҲ’ M = 2n + 1, (48) where n вүҘ 0 is an integer. In Appendix C, we discuss more details of the multipole coupling function through the expansion of the Legendre polynomial in a Fourier basis. C. Bin-averaging effect In real observations, we measure the signal of the 3PCF in binned triangles, which means the underlying signal is averaged within each bin of the measurement. This effect alters the scale dependence and triangle shape depen- dence of the signal. For a robust comparison of theory to the measured signal to extract unbiased cosmologi- cal/astrophysical parameters, we need to take into ac- count the bin-averaging effect on the theory side as well. The bin averaging effect on theoretical prediction is formulated as ВҜО“Г— 0 (Оё1i, Оё2j, П•k) = Z Оё1i d lnОё1 Оё2 1 A1i Z Оё2j d lnОё2 Оё2 2 A2j Z П•k dП• вҲҶП•k О“Г— 0 (Оё1, Оё2, П•) (49) where i, j and k are the labels of the Оё1, Оё2 and П• bins respectively. The integration runs over Оё1 вҲҲ [Оё1i,min, Оё1i,max], Оё2 вҲҲ [Оё2j,min, Оё2j,max], and П• вҲҲ [П•k,min, П•k,max], and A1i = ( Оё2 1i,max вҲ’ Оё2 1i,min)/2, A2j = (Оё2 2j,max вҲ’ Оё2 2j,min)/2, and вҲҶП•k = П•k,max вҲ’ П•k,min. The bin-averaging operator on the angle П• acting on the basis eiMП• in Eq. (37) can be performed analytically, and the result is equivalent to replacing eiMП• вҶ’ eiMП•k,min eiMвҲҶП•k вҲ’ 1 iMвҲҶП•k . (50) Similar the averaging effect on Оё1 and Оё2 can be easily taken into account by replacing the basis of 2D-FFTLog. While we do not go into detail about the 2D-FFTLog algorithm, we briefly summarize it to show how the av- eraging effect can be taken into account. The algorithm basically expands the target function as the sum of a double-power law with coefficients as О“Г—,(M) 0 (Оё1, Оё2) = X p,q cpqОёвҲ’iО·pвҲ’ОҪ1 1 ОёвҲ’iО·qвҲ’ОҪ2 2 (51) where p and q are the multipole indices, and О·p, О·q, ОҪ1, ОҪ2 are the real values. Then the action of bin-averaging operators is equivalent to replacing ОёвҲ’iО·pвҲ’ОҪ1 1 вҶ’ ОёвҲ’iО·pвҲ’ОҪ1 1i,min s(2 вҲ’ iО·p вҲ’ ОҪ1, вҲҶln x1i) s(2, вҲҶln x1i) (52) TABLE I. This is already implemented in the public code, and we refer the reader to [73] for the complete formalism of 2D-FFTLog. In this way, the bin-averaging effect can be taken into account without any additional computational cost. Note that the way to incorporate the bin-averaging ef- fect by replacing the bases with the bin-averaged ones described above is only valid for the multipole expansion with Г—-projection. In a different projection, we need a phase factor to convert from Г—-projection to the pro- jection under consideration, and it breaks the form of the component to be expressed with analytic bases be- cause the multiplied phase factor depends on the triangle. For this reason, Г—-projection has the advantage that it can include the bin-average effect without any additional computational cost compared to other choice of projec- tions. IV. RESULTS A. For the sake of computational efficiency, we use interpolations of bispectrum and bispectrum multipoles in the multipole expansion and multipole coupling operations respectively. ferent shear projection P, we multiply the corresponding phase factor to convert the Г—-projection to the desired projection P. Table I summarize the fiducial choice of FFT grid pa- rameters. For 2D-FFTLog, we use 200 2 FFT grid values in (в„“1, в„“2) and ( Оё1, Оё2). The range of the FFT grid de- pends on the model of the bispectrum. For the case of BiHalofit that we use in this paper, we found the scales в„“12 вҲҲ [10вҲ’1, 105] capture the most significant bis- pectrum signals, and hence we use в„“12,min = 10 вҲ’1 and в„“12,max = 105 as the fiducial choice. The algorithm of 2D-FFTLog has its own hyper pa- rameters, the bias parameters ОҪi (i=1,2) and the width of the window c window width. The former is the real part of the power law of the Fourier expansion and its choice is arbitrary as long as the following condition is satisfied: the bias parameter must be вҲ’|m| < ОҪi < 2 for the Hankel transformation with Jm(Оёiв„“i), and ОҪi must be non-integer. The latter hyper parameter controls the width of window multiplied on the Fourier coefficients in the unit of Nyquist frequency, which is introduced to re- duce the ringing effect in [74]. In this paper, we follow the default choice of 2D-FFTLog for these parameters as summarized in Table I, which we found gives stable results. In Section III, we introduced the multipole expansion of triangles in real and Fourier spaces. For the practical implementation of Eqs. (39) and (37), we need to trun- cate the multipole summation at a finite maximum mul- tipole L вүӨ Lmax and |M| вүӨMmax. The choice of Lmax and Mmax is a trade-off between the computational cost and the accuracy of the prediction. We use Lmax = 30 and Mmax = 30 as the fiducial choice, which we found gives a robust estimate of the 3PCF as we will see in Sec- tion IV C. We note that this choice of Lmax and Mmax is tested for BiHalofit, and they can be optimized differ- ently for the other specific model of bispectrum and the desired accuracy of the prediction. We implemented the formalism with Python using the implementation of 2D-FFTLog available at [75]. The code developed in this paper is publicly available on GitHub[76] and installable with pip[77] or conda[78]. In Fig. 5, we demonstrate the method developed in this paper based on multipole expansion and 2D-FFTLog. Using this code, we can evaluate a 3PCF within вҲј 10 seconds. While we show only the real part of the 3PCFs and for specific choice of П• values in this plot for simplic- ity, the imaginary part and those for other П• values are also obtained at the same time without any extra run- time. We can also clearly see the symmetry properties of Eqs. (21) and (22) by comparing the lowest and second lowest panels. The ease of the visualization of the shear 3PCF as a function of two side lengths Оё1 and Оё2 plus the opening angle П• is an important advantage of this method for understand the dependence on the triangle configuration, as well as the speed of the evaluation of the shear 3PCF. B. Bispectrum multipole expansion Accurate estimation of bispectrum multipoles is an im- portant part of this formalism. We find that the naive discretization of Eq. (29), i.e. approximation with Rie- mann sum, fails, and gives artificially higher multipoles depending on the choice of number of bins on the open- ing angle, cos Оұ. To overcome this difficulty, we instead use the interpolation-coefficient based approach. Based on the fact that the bispectrum of interest is usually a smooth function, we can sufficiently capture the func- tional form of the bispectrum with a small number of sampling points on the opening angle Оұ and their inter- polation. Once the functional form of the bispectrum is given with the interpolation coefficients, which are calcu- lated from the small number of sampling points, we can analytically perform the multipole expansion in Eq. (29) and then the result is stable to the choice of bin number as long as the number of bins is large enough to capture the opening-angle dependence of the bispectrum. In addition to the discretization of the multipole ex- pansion described above, we need to be careful about the choice of sampling points on the cosine of the open- ing angle, cos Оұ. The bispectrum has very sharp peak at cos Оұ вҲј 1 when в„“1 вҲј в„“2, because this domain corresponds to the squeezed-limit triangle which is known to give high amplitude of the bispectrum. Therefore, a linearly equally spaced bins on cos Оұ fails to capture the sharp peak at cos Оұ вҲј 1 and consequently gives smaller ampli- tude of multipoles. To obtain unbiased bispectrum mul- tipoles, taking care of such squeezed-limit triangles, we combine two different binning choices: linearly equally- spaced bins over Вө вүЎ cos Оұ вҲҲ [вҲ’1, 1 вҲ’ 0.05] and logarith- mically equally-spaced bins over Вө вҲҲ [1 вҲ’ 0.05, 1 вҲ’ ПөВө], where ПөВө determines the most squeezed-limit triangle taken into account. (29), we need to evaluate the bispectrum for various triangle configurations ( в„“1, в„“2, Оұ). To speed up the com- putation, we use an interpolation function for evaluating the bispectrum multipoles. We also note that our in- terpolating function parametrizes the triangles using the ruv notation introduced in [71] rather than SAS nota- tion. In the SAS parametrization, a given triangle shows up multiple times with different parameters, depending on which vertex is taken to be the вҖңAвҖқ vertex. The ruv parametrization only includes each triangle once, so it avoids this redundancy. We also use interpolation for bispectrum multipoles b(L) Оә (в„“1, в„“2) for the sake of numerical efficiency. Again to avoid redundancy of the bispectrum multipole evalu- ation, we use the ( в„“, ПҲ) notation with 0 < ПҲ < ПҖ/4 of the bispectrum multipole rather than ( в„“1, в„“2) notation. The interpolation of bispectrum multipole is done on the (в„“, ПҲ) grid. Fig. 6 shows the performance of the bispectrum multi- pole expansion. To evaluate the performance, we recon- structed the bispectrum as a function of cos Оұ from the bispectrum multipoles using Eq. (28) up to Lmax = 30 and compared it to the original bispectrum. The left panel shows the bispectrum for equilateral triangles with varying scale в„“, and the right panel shows the bispec- trum for fixed-side triangles with varying opening angle Оұ. We find that the bispectrum reconstructed from the bispectrum multipoles obtained by the multipole expan- sion is in good agreement with the original bispectrum, at the level of 3% accuracy. For the isosceles triangles, в„“1 вҲј в„“2, the residuals of the reconstructed bispectrum sometimes exceed 3%. We found that this is due to leak- age of the higher multipoles which tries to fit the steep increase of the bispectrum toward Оұ вҶ’ 0 for squeezed limit triangles. The figure also shows the error of the bispectrum interpolation, where we can see that the er- ror is mostly introduced in the interpolation step. Here we use 35 Г— 35 Г— 25 bins on ruv for bispectrum interpo- lation. We can achieve higher accuracy by using a finer grid on ruv at the expense of computational efficiency. C. Efficiency of evaluation In this section, we discuss the efficiency of the eval- uation of the 3PCF with the multipole method devel- oped in this paper. To assess the computational effi- ciency, we measure the execution time on a MacBook Air 1.1 GHz Quad-Core Intel Core i5 processor. Because we are interested in the efficiency for Monte-Carlo sam- pling application, we remove the computational time for the initialization of the code, e.g. the times for the ini-12 tializing the FFT/interpolation grid, pre-computing the multipole coupling function, and so on. We divided the remaining computation into 9 steps: cosmo, z-kernel, interp bk, interp bkL, multipole, HM, GammaM, Gamma, and projection. The cosmo and z-kernel steps com- pute the redshift-distance relation and lensing kernels in Eq. (2) from a given set of redshift bins. The interp bk and interp bkL steps interpolate the bispectrum and the bispectrum multipole on the FFT grid. The multipole step finds the bispectrum multipoles from the interpo- lation. The HM step obtains the kernel function HM by summing the products of the bispectrum multipoles and the multipole coupling functions up to Lmax. The GammaM step computes the 3PCF multipoles by perform- ing the double Hankel transformation with 2D-FFTLog for all |M| < Mmax. The Gamma step finds the real space 3PCF by summing the multipoles up to Mmax. The projection step converts the 3PCF from Г—-projection to the centroid projection. We first consider the simplest version of the analy- sis with just a single source redshift bin. The first col- umn of Table II summarize the computational time. In this case, the interp bk dominates the total computa- tional time. In this step, we compute the convergence bispectrum performing the line-of-sight integration in Eq. (12). Even though we reduce the redundancy of triangle parametrization using ruv notation, this step still requires the evaluation of the matter bispectrum on 35 Г— 35 Г— 25 Г— 55 вүҲ 106 sampling points for r, u, v and z. The next slowest steps are interp bkL and HM. These steps include only simple operations such as in- terpolation and linear algebra on an FFT grid, but we repeat the same operation for all the multipoles under consideration Lmax times and Mmax times respectively; hence they can be computationally expensive compared to other steps. As expected, the GammaM step, where we perform the double Hankel transformation, is not a dom- inant part of this formalism thanks to the efficiency of 2D-FFTLog. The total computational time for the sin- gle source redshift bin is вҲј 7 seconds, which is of order 106 times faster than the brute-force integration method, which takes more than вҲј 1 minute for a single triangle configuration and thus around 10 5 minutes in total. In weak lensing analysis, it is common to use tomogra- phy to probe the growth history of large-scale structure. When we consider a tomographic setup with n source redshift bins, then we will have n(n + 1)(n + 2)/6 auto- and cross-redshift bin combinations, and we need to per- form the calculations of the 3PCF for each of these. The second and third columns of Table II show the measured computational costs for the cases of n = 2 and 4. The computational time of the interp bk step is not very affected by the number of source redshift bins, because we reuse the evaluated matter bispectrum for the calcu- lation of the convergence bispectrum, and the additional computational cost is only to perform the line-of-sight integration for additional source redshift bin combina- tions.
